coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive label-free fluorescence determination of lymphotropic virus DNA based on exonuclease assisted target recycling amplification and in-situ generation of fluorescent copper nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Junyang
Wang, Mengke
Zhou, Xiaobin
Nie, Yixin
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: Specific and sensitive detection of target DNA plays a significant role in clinical diagnosis and biomedical research. Herein, a sensitive ratio fluorescence biosensor was constructed based on in-situ generation of fluorescent copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) on the DNA modified graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the detection of HTLV-I DNA. Firstly, large amount of AT rich single DNA (o-DNA) can be released from the designed hairpin DNA in the presence of trace target DNA via exonuclease III assisted target recycling amplification strategy. Then, the released o-DNA hybridized with the complementary DNA1 which was modified on the surface of GQDs, and the formed hybridized DNA with blunt 3′-hydroxylated terminus that couldn’t be digested by exonuclease I could act as the template for the generation of fluorescent CuNCs. While in the absence of target DNA, the o-DNA couldn’t be released, thus the DNA1 with 3′overhang ends could be degraded by exonuclease I, which resulted in the in-situ generation of fluorescent CuNCs on the surface of GQDs was blocked owing to the lack of DNA template. The GQD fluorescence in the GQD-CuNC nanohybrid was almost uninfluenced during the whole process. Therefore, with the GQD serving as the reference and CuNC acting as the reporter signal, there was an excellent linear relationship between the change of fluorescence intensity ratio (F595/F445) and the concentration of HTLV-I DNA in the range of 20 pM-12 nM with a detection limit of 10 pM. Furthermore, the biosensor exhibited satisfactory results for monitoring the presence of HTLV-I DNA in human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Graphene quantum dots
GQD-CuNC nanohybrid
Ratio fluorescence
HTLV-I DNA

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of single strand DNA1 modified GQDs
Synthesis of CuNCs and GQD-CuNC nanohybrid
Characterization of CuNCs, GQDs, and DNA modified GQDs
Detection mechanism and feasibility for HTLV-I DNA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration displayed the mechanism for the in-situ detection of HTLV-I DNA.

(A) TEM images of CuNCs. Inset displayed HRTEM images of CuNCs and size distribution of CuNCs. (B) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission, excitation spectra of CuNCs. (C) TEM images of GQDs. Inset displayed the size distribution of GQDs. (D) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission, excitation spectra of GQDs.
(A) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of the bare GCE electrode, GQDs/GCE, GQD-ssDNA/GCE, and GQD-sdsDNA/GCE. (B) The emission and excitation spectra of GQDs, GQD-ssDNA, and GQD-sdsDNA.

(A) TEM images of GQD-CuNC nanohybrid. (B) Fluorescence spectra of the GQD-DNA1/h-DNA/Exo III/ascorbate/Cu2+ sensing system treated with or without Exo I. (C) Fluorescence spectra of GQD-ssDNA/h-DNA/Exo III/Exo I/ascorbate/ Cu2+ sensing system treated with or without t-DNA.

The effects of (A) Cu2+ concentration and (B) sodium ascorbate concentration on the fluorescence intensity ratio of F595/F445. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the sensing system with various concentrations of HTLV-I DNA (From bottom to top: 0, 0.02, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 and 15 nM, respectively). (D) The linearity of fluorescence intensity ratio F595/F445 versus concentrations of HTLV-I DNA.

(A) Fluorescence intensity variation at 445 and 595 nm of the sensing system with 10 nM target DNA as a function of time under 340 nm UV illumination. (B)The selectivity of system for HTLV-I DNA assay. From left to right: (a) blank, (b) 15 nM two-base-mismatched DNA, (c) 15 nM one-base-mismatched DNA, and (d) 15 nM target DNA.
Analytical results of human serum sample with spiked HTLV-I DNA.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive label-free fluorescence determination of lymphotropic virus DNA based on exonuclease assisted target recycling amplification and in-situ generation of fluorescent copper nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electron Microscopy Center, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electron Microscopy Center, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration displayed the mechanism for the in-situ detection of HTLV-I DNA.

Specific and sensitive detection of target DNA plays a significant role in clinical diagnosis and biomedical research. Herein, a sensitive ratio fluorescence biosensor was constructed based on in-situ generation of fluorescent copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) on the DNA modified graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the detection of HTLV-I DNA. Firstly, large amount of AT rich single DNA (o-DNA) can be released from the designed hairpin DNA in the presence of trace target DNA via exonuclease III assisted target recycling amplification strategy. Then, the released o-DNA hybridized with the complementary DNA1 which was modified on the surface of GQDs, and the formed hybridized DNA with blunt 3′-hydroxylated terminus that couldn’t be digested by exonuclease I could act as the template for the generation of fluorescent CuNCs. While in the absence of target DNA, the o-DNA couldn’t be released, thus the DNA1 with 3′overhang ends could be degraded by exonuclease I, which resulted in the in-situ generation of fluorescent CuNCs on the surface of GQDs was blocked owing to the lack of DNA template. The GQD fluorescence in the GQD-CuNC nanohybrid was almost uninfluenced during the whole process. Therefore, with the GQD serving as the reference and CuNC acting as the reporter signal, there was an excellent linear relationship between the change of fluorescence intensity ratio (F595/F445) and the concentration of HTLV-I DNA in the range of 20 pM-12 nM with a detection limit of 10 pM. Furthermore, the biosensor exhibited satisfactory results for monitoring the presence of HTLV-I DNA in human serum.

ce:section.ce:para: Numerous biosensors have been developed over the past decades for specific detection of biomolecule to meet the increasing demands for early medical diagnostics and point-of-care treatment of diseases [1,2]. DNA biosensors are especially crucial since DNA detection plays a significant role in mutation analysis, gene therapy, forensic samples analysis, pathogen detection, and bioterrorism agents screening [3–5]. Human T-cell lymphotropic virus type I (HTLV-I), a human retrovirus, is the causative agent of adult T-cell leukemia-lymphoma, which is associated with the cancers in human and can mainly cause two diseases including HTLV-I-associated myelopathy/tropical spastic paraparesis (HAM/TSP) and adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma [6,7]. Thus, it is of great demands for exploit some effective and sensitive methods to trace the presence of HTLV-I at the earlier time. Therefore, the development of direct nucleic acid-based techniques which can trace the presence of DNA sequence related to HTLV-I has aroused researchers’ interest [8,9].

Up to now, several techniques have been exploited for the detection of specific DNA sequences, including electrochemiluminescence, Surface-enhanced Raman Scattering (SERS), high performance liquid chromatography, colorimetric, and ﬂuorescence-based assay [10–14]. Among them, ﬂuorescence-based biosensors are especially stand out owing to their high sensitivity, easy operability, and feasibility of quantiﬁcation. Li et al. developed a target-catalyzed hairpin assembly (CHA) method utilizing a hairpin DNA labeled with fluorophore and quencher at two ends to sensor specific DNA [15]. Chowdhury proposed a dye labeled DNA based on gold nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots system for quantify DNA [16]. Although great progress for DNA detection have achieved, the fluorescence responses were mainly dependent to the distance between ﬂuorophore and quencher suffering from high background, high cost, and toxicity of organic reagents, which may hinder their further application. Consequently, the fabrication of a feasible fluorescent biosensor for effective and sensitive detection of DNA with high signal-to-noise ratio, label-free, low-cost is highly desired.

Fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been extensively used as ideal alternatives for semiconductor quantum dots in the application of ﬂuorescent detection and bioimaging owing to the virtues of good water-solubility, excellent biocompatibility, and better surface modification [17–22]. Newly emerged DNA templated copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) have been utilized as excellent novel fluorescent makers, which exhibit distinctive properties such as easy and fast synthesis, the large Stokes shifting (∼260 nm), low cost, and preferable biocompatibility [23,24]. Besides, the synthesis for DNA templated CuNCs was completed in several minutes, which was time-saving and more propitious for in-situ generation the point-of-care fluorescent reporter signal for biochemical analysis compared with silver nanoclusters (12 h in dark) [25] and gold nanoclusters (2 days) [26]. Fluorescent CuNCs were selectively formed on double stranded DNA (dsDNA) and adenine-thymine (AT)-rich sequences were preferred templates for highly fluorescent CuNC compared with random dsDNA [27,28]. Subsequently, polyT ssDNA were also discovered as efficient templates for synthesize CuNC by Wang and coworkers [29]. Based on above reports, some biosensors have been designed on the basis of polyT ssDNA or dsDNA templated CuNCs. Zhou developed an aptamer consisting dsDNA as templates for preparation of CuNCs for ATP detection [30]; Through introducing the hybridization chain reaction (HCR), Qu’s group successfully applied the elongated dsDNA as the templates for preparation CuNCs to selectively sensor cancer cells and microRNA [31]. Li and coworkers utilized polyT templated CuNCs for detection of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) in real samples [32]. However, the above sensing platforms were based on polyT ssDNA or dsDNA templated CuNCs, and the construction of biosensors through combining polyT ssDNA and dsDNA as the template for the synthesis of CuNCs were still limited. Although, the dumbbell-shaped DNAs were elaborately designed by grafting two poly T-loop on the dsDNA, which served as the perfect template to enhance the fluorescence of CuNCs and were further utilized to effectively detect ligase and polynucleotide kinase (PNK) [33], the construction of hairpin or dumbbell-shaped DNAs need ingenious and sophisticated design, which restrict their further applications. Thus, the biosensors constructed through the simple combination of polyT ssDNA and dsDNA as the preferred template for CuNCs were urgently needed to further development. Furthermore, considering the fascinating properties of GQDs and DNA templated CuNCs, it is quite promising for the integration of fluorescent GQDs and CuNCs to construct the ratio fluorescence probe for the detection of DNA.

Herein, we designed a specific single-double-single strand DNA (sdsDNA) template [34], which was coming from the hybridization of DNA1 and DNA2 (sequence details see in Table S1), for the in-situ preparation of the fluorescence enhanced CuNCs (Scheme S1). Utilizing the fluorescence enhanced CuNCs and fluorescent GQDs, a ratio double-signal fluorescent probe was built to sensor DNA which can effectively improve the accuracy due to their built-in self-calibration and elimination of environmental inﬂuences. As displayed in Scheme 1 , DNA1 was utilized to modify GQD with blue fluorescent emission. The hairpin DNA (h-DNA, sequence details see in Table S1) consists of two regions. The first one was recognition region which can be recognized by t-DNA (HTLV-I, sequence details see in Table S1) at 3’ terminal, and the other one was output DNA (o-DNA) which could be partially hybridized with DNA1 modified on GQDs. In the presence of t-DNA, h-DNA could form the dsDNA with blunt 3’-terminus which could be digested by exonuclease III (Exo III) and then released o-DNA and t-DNA. The released t-DNA can participate the next circles and released numerous o-DNA, while the released o-DNA was captured by DNA1 on the GQDs. After hybridization, the formed sdsDNA on the GQD with blunt 3’ terminus couldn’t be digested by exonuclease I (Exo I), which could be served as template for in-situ production of fluorescence CuNCs as reporter signal. However, the o-DNA couldn’t be released out without t-DNA and then DNA1 with overhanging 3’ terminus would be digested by Exo I, which result the lack of template for in-situ generation of fluorescent CuNCs. In addition, the fluorescence of GQDs was almost not influenced in the whole process. Thus, the presence of HTLV-I DNA could be directly monitored via the change of fluorescence intensity ratio (F595/F445). In this work, we not only discovered for the first time that sdsDNA as template could enhance the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs, but also utilized the enhanced CuNC as the reporter signal, thus improving the detection sensitivity. Besides, the Exo III assisted target recycling amplification strategy was introduced to greatly improve the sensitivity and selectivity, and Exo I was innovatively utilized in the detection system which can largely decrease the background signal and further increase the detection sensitivity. In addition, our sensing system was label-free and the reporter signal was come from in-situ generation of CuNC without extra fluorescence dye and quencher label, which greatly simplified detection procedures. Moreover, the utilization of GQD-CuNC hybrids in our work not only constructed ratio dual-signal fluorescent probe which avoided the influence of intrinsic and environmental fluorescence, but also increased the detection sensitivity in comparison with the single fluorescence probe of CuNCs. The designed sensor system exhibited remarkable analytical performance, which has promising applications for bioanalysis and clinical diagnosis.

Copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O), ammonia (NH3·H2O, 25.0–28.0 %), 1-ethyl-3-[3-di-methylaminopropyl] carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and sodium ascorbate were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Exonuclease I (20 U/μL) and Exonuclease III (200 U/μL) were bought from Sangon Biotech Co. and Thermo Fisher Scientific Co. respectively. The DNA were synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotech Co. The details of DNA sequence and instruments were supplied in the Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of single strand DNA1 modified GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were prepared according to previous reports [35]. Briefly, 0.016 g citric acid, 4 mL ultrapure H2O, and 2 mL ammonia solution were successively added to 20 mL vital and mixed well. Then, the obtained solution was added to a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and subsequently heated at 200 °C about 3 h. After the solution was cooled down to room temperature, it was purified in a dialysis membrane (8000−14000 Da) with distilled water for 16 h to remove the impurities. The purified GQDs solution was stored in refrigerator at 4 °C for further application.

DNA1 modified GQDs (GQD-ssDNA) was synthesized based on condensation reaction between the carboxyl groups and amino groups. Typically, 200 μL EDC (4 mg mL−1) was added to 240 μL GQDs (50 times diluted) in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) and the mixture was stirred for 30 min to activate carboxyl. And then 200 μL DNA1 (10 μM) was injected to the mixture and reacted for overnight with continuous stirring. The GQD-ssDNA conjugates was obtained after dialysis, which was stored at 4 °C refrigerator for further investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CuNCs and GQD-CuNC nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: DNA-CuNC were prepared according previous reports [30]. Briefly, the template DNA were first hybridized at room temperature for 15 min, then PBS buffer (20 mM PBS, 100 mM NaCl, 1 mM MgCl2, pH 7.6) was added to dilute the template DNA (250 nM). After 15 μL 20 mM sodium ascorbate was added and mixed completely, 5 μL 5 mM CuSO4 was added and incubated at room temperature to form CuNCs. The GQD-CuNC nanohybrid was synthesized based on above procedure, except for the template changed to be GQD-DNA.

Prior to EIS experiment, the bare GCE was polished by 1.0 and 0.05 μm α-Al2O3 powder on rough paper and then ultrasonically cleaned in ethanol and distilled water to get a mirror-like surface. The cleaned GCE was immersed in (0.5 %) PSS (Poly(sodium-p-styrenesulfonate)) solution for 5 min and then 8 μL GQDs/GQD-ssDNA/GQD-sdsDNA dropped on PSS/GCE and dried at room temperature. Finally, EIS experiment was conducted in 0.1 mol/L KCl solution containing 2.5 mmol/L K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6].

The 20 % polyacrylamide gel was utilized for the native electrophoresis analysis and ran at 1 × TBE, 100 mM NaCl buffer solution at 70 V constant voltage at room temperature for 12 h. After stained by Gel-Green in 100 mM NaCl, the gel was scanned by ultrapower visible light transilluminator to observe the results and the photos were taken by phone.

Firstly, different amount of t-DNA was incubated with the solution containing 12 μL h-DNA (10 μM) and 8 μL Exo III (2U/μL) for 60 min at 37 °C to produce o-DNA. After the solution mixture was heated at 75 °C for 10 min to inactivate Exo III, 32 μL GQD-ssDNA was added to the cooled mixture and incubated for another 15 min. Then, the mixture was incubated with 10 μL Exo I (1U/μL) for 30 min to digest the 3’ overhang ssDNA. Finally, 15 μL 20 mM sodium ascorbate and 5 μL 5 mM CuSO4 were added to the mixture with PBS buffer (20 mM PBS, 100 mM NaCl, 1 mM MgCl2, pH 7.6) for making 400 μL final volume and the fluorescence spectra were acquired with the excitation of 340 nm after incubated for 10 min.

Human blood samples were obtained from the Changchun China-Japan Union Hospital. The blood samples were firstly centrifugated for 15 min at the speed of 10,000 rpm before analysis, and the obtained supernatant liquid was diluted 10 times with distilled water. Then, different concentrations of HTLV-I DNA were added to the above solution to prepare spiked samples. The detection protocol for HTLV-I in the spiked samples was similar with the procedure stated above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CuNCs, GQDs, and DNA modified GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The characterization of CuNCs, GQDs, and DNA modified GQDs was achieved by TEM, fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). TEM images (Fig. 1 A, C) displayed that both CuNCs and GQDs were uniform and well monodispersed, and the average diameter of CuNCs and GQDs were 4.78 nm and 2.00 nm, respectively. HRTEM images of CuNCs (Fig. 1A inset) revealed the lattice spacing distance was 1.3 Å, which was consistent with the (220) plane of face-centered cubic Cu [36]. As shown in Fig. 1B, the maximum emission of CuNCs was centered at 595 nm with maximum excitation at 350 nm. The absorption peak of CuNCs was centered around 350 nm, which was in accordance with the excitation peak. Fig. 1D exhibited that the maximum excitation and emission of GQDs were centered at 350 nm and 445 nm respectively. The characteristic UV–vis absorption peak of GQDs was at 340 nm. GQDs were further characterized by the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1) to investigate the surface functional groups. The characteristic peaks of CO at 1384 cm−1, CO at 1618 cm−1 and OH at 3417 cm-1 indicated the presence of carboxyl on the surface of GQDs [35,37]. Thus, the condensation reaction between the carboxyl groups on GQDs and the amino groups modified ssDNA could be occurred, and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was applied to demonstrate the successful modification of ssDNA on GQDs. As displayed in Fig. S2, after the modification of DNA1 on the surface of GQDs, the appearance of the absorption peak around 220 nm and 260 nm that was ascribed to amide bond and DNA1 respectively, which indicated the successful synthesis of GQD-ssDNA. The resistance of electron transfer (Ret) of the modified electrodes could be reflected by the diameter of semicircle. As shown in Fig. 2 A, The Ret value of the GCE increased from 105.7 Ω to 181.4 Ω after the modification of GQDs. After the modification of ssDNA on GQDs (GQD-ssDNA), the electron transfer resistance increased to 231.2 Ω, which was due to DNA without conductive properties and electrostatic repulsion between phosphate skeleton of DNA and Fe (CN)6 3- / 4- [38]. The Ret value further increased to 318.3 Ω after GQD-sdsDNA was modified on GCE. The results suggested the successful conjugation of DNA1 on the surface of GQDs. Fig. 2B showed that the fluorescence spectra of GQD-ssDNA and GQD-sdsDNA kept identical to that of GQDs.

Poly (AT-TA) dsDNA and poly T ssDNA had been proved as efficient template for synthesis fluorescent CuNCs. Besides, Lu’s group discovered that random dsDNA with poly T tail as template could dramatically increase the fluorescence intensity of CuNC because polyT could promote the generation of CuNC on random dsDNA when they were linked together [39]. Based on previous reports, we studied whether poly (AT-TA) dsDNA linked with random ssDNA could enhance the fluorescence of CuNCs. As shown in Fig. S3A and Scheme S1, after hybridization of DNA2 and DNA4 (sequence details see in Table S1), the AT rich dsDNA linked with random ssDNA promoted the generation of CuNCs, and the fluorescence was slightly increased. In order to study whether poly (AT-TA) dsDNA with polyT and random ssDNA tails could further enhance the fluorescence of CuNCs, we designed two DNA sequences including DNA1 and DNA2 (Scheme S1). After the two DNA hybridized, the formed DNA (sdsDNA) containing poly (AT-TA) dsDNA linked with poly T and random ssDNA tails. Remarkably, the enhanced fluorescence CuNC was acquired when sdsDNA as template in comparison with polyT ssDNA or poly (AT-TA) dsDNA as template (Fig. S3B). Thus, we utilize the fluorescence enhanced phenomenon of CuNC templated by sdsDNA in the HTLV-I DNA assay system, which can improve the detection sensitivity. Furthermore, Fig. 3 B displayed that the use of Exo I in in-situ generation of CuNC can greatly decreased the background signal at 595 nm and got high signal-to-noise ratio, which was also beneficial to increase the detection sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism and feasibility for HTLV-I DNA
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 shows the ratio fluorescence system based on GQD-CuNC nanohybrid for the detection of HTLV-I DNA. The ssDNA modified GQDs could hybridize with o-DNA that came from Exo III assisted target recycling amplification process. After hybridization, the formed sdsDNA template on the surface of GQDs could not be digested by Exo I, and can in-situ generation fluorescence enhanced CuNC on GQDs in the presence of sodium ascorbate and Cu2+. Without target HTLV-I DNA, the GQD-sdsDNA could not generated owing to the lack of o-DNA, and the ssDNA on GQDs can be degraded by Exo I and resulted in the lack of template for in-situ generation of fluorescence enhanced CuNC on GQDs. Thus, HTLV-I DNA can be detected via the fluorescence intensity change of FCuNCs/FGQDs in the ratio fluorescence system. TEM images (Fig. 3A) showed the successful synthesis of GQD-CuNC nanohybrid with good dispersion.

The mechanism for Exo III assisted target recycling amplification process and DNA hybridization were verified by native polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) experiment. As shown in Fig. S4, after h-DNA treated with Exo III in the presence of t-DNA (line 4), the band of h-DNA was significantly weakened compared the standard sample of h-DNA (line 2) and a new bright band that is same like DNA2 band (line 3) was appear, which indicated the o-DNA that was same like DNA2 was produced after Exo III assisted target recycling amplification. The successful hybridization between DNA1 and o-DNA was verified by line 6 which had significantly weaker band than line 2 because of h-DNA digested by Exo III in the recycling amplification process and had a new band same as DNA1-DNA2 hybridization (line 7) owing to DNA1 hybridized with o-DNA generating sdsDNA. To further investigate the feasibility of the strategy for the detection of HTLV-I DNA, fluorescence emission spectra of the system was performed in the presence or absence of HTLV-I DNA. As shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity of CuNC at 595 nm was very weak in the absence of HTLV-I DNA, while the fluorescence intensity at 595 nm was significantly increased in the presence of HTLV-I DNA and the fluorescence emission of GQDs at 445 nm was almost identical in the process. Besides, Fig. S5 showed that the influence of GQDs to the formation of CuNC can be neglectable. These results verified the feasibility of the designed ratio fluorescence platform based on GQD-CuNC nanohybrid for HTLV-I DNA assay.

In order to improve the performance of the platform for HTLV-I DNA analysis, several vital factors including the concentration of Cu2+ and sodium ascorbate, reaction time, and the amount of Exo I and Exo III were optimized. Fig. 4 A shows the fluorescence intensity ratio of CuNCs and GQD (F595/F445) increased with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, and then slowly decreased at higher concentration of 0.0625 mM Cu2+. The fluorescence intensity ratio F595/F445 increased with the concentration of sodium ascorbate increase and kept stable over 0.75 mM sodium ascorbate (Fig. 4B). Thus, 0.0625 mM and 0.75 mM were selected as optimal concentration for Cu2+ and sodium ascorbate. The reaction time between Cu2+ and sodium ascorbate for the formation of GQD-CuNC nanohybrid was optimized and 10 min was selected as reaction time (Fig. S6A). The fluorescence intensity ratio of F595/F445 gradually increased with the increase of Exo III concentration and kept unchangeable when Exo III was more than 16 U (Fig. S6B). The fluorescence intensity ratio of F595/F445 (Fig. S6C) was rapidly decreased with Exo I increase and then reached stable when the amount of Exo I was 10 U. Therefore, 16 U Exo III and 10 U Exo I was chosen in the following experiments. The reaction time of Exo III and Exo I (Fig. S6D–E) was also optimized, which indicated that 60 min and 30 min was the optimized reaction time, respectively.

Under the optimized conditions, the GQD-CuNC nanohybrid ratio fluorescence system was used for the detection of HTLV-I DNA. Fig. 4C showed the fluorescence spectra of the assay system with various concentrations of HTLV-I DNA. It can be seen from Fig. 4C, with the increase of HTLV-I DNA concentrations, the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs at 595 nm gradually increased whereas GQD fluorescence intensity at 445 nm maintained constant. As exhibited in Fig. 4D, a good lineal relationship was obtained between the fluorescence intensity ratio F595/F445 and the concentrations of HTLV-I DNA and the linear regression equation was F595/F445 = 0.299 + 0.158 Ct-DNA in the ranges of 20 pM–12 nM (R2 = 0.988). The detection limit (LOD) for HTLV-I DNA was 10 pM based on 3σ/k (where σ was the standard deviation of blank signals (n = 11) of the sensing system and k was the slope of fluorescence intensity ratio F595/F445 versus HTLV-I DNA concentration). Besides, the stability of the biosensor (Fig. 5 A) for HTLV-I DNA detection was investigated by measuring the fluorescence intensity changes of the detecting system with 10 nM target DNA, and the fluorescence intensity at 445 nm and 595 nm kept identical after exposure of UV illumination at 340 nm for 20 min, which indicated the biosensor have excellent stability. Furthermore, copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) as single fluorescence probe was utilized to sensing the t-DNA. As displayed in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system was kept increased with the increase of t-DNA concentrations, and a liner relation (F/F0 = 1.322 + 0.689 Ct-DNA (R2 = 0.987) in the ranges of 50 pM–12 nM with the LOD of 28 pM) was acquired, where F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity of the biosensor in the presence and absence of t-DNA respectively. The obtained results illustrated that the ratio fluorescence probe of GQD-CuNC had higher sensitivity in comparison with the single fluorescence probe of CuNCs. In addition, the overview of previous reports for DNA determination based on fluorescence method was listed in Table S2, which demonstrated our method displayed comparable quantification range and LOD.

The selectivity of an assay method is a crucial factor to evaluate the analytical performance. In this study, the fluorescence response to 10 nM target DNA, one-base mismatched DNA and two-base mismatched DNA were investigated under the same conditions to assess the specificity of the method. As shown in Fig. 5B, one-base mismatched DNA displayed a small F595/F445 change and two-base mismatched DNA showed negligible change of F595/F445 compared with the blank sample, whereas the addition of target HTLV-I DNA could significantly increase fluorescence intensity ratio F595/F445. The results suggested that our method possessed high specificity for the detection of target DNA.

To verify the practicability and reliability of the present strategy for HTLV-I DNA detection, the human serum samples spiked with various concentrations of HTLV-I DNA were measured. Fig. S8 showed the interference of human serum (10 %) on the fluorescence of the sensing system was negligible. Table 1 showed that the average recoveries of target DNA were in the range of 92.00–107.50% with the RSD less than 6.15 %. For comparison, the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) method [40] was used to detect the spiked samples, and the results were consistent with that obtained by our method, which indicated the feasibility of our strategy for HTLV-I DNA detection in real samples.

In summary, a ratio fluorescence biosensor was constructed based on in-situ generation of GQD-CuNC nanohybrid favored by exonuclease III assisted target recycling amplification method for the detection of HTLV-I DNA. Benefiting from exonuclease III assisted target recycling amplification as well as exonuclease I assisted cleavage reaction for the decrease of background signal, the assay exhibited excellent sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of HTLV-I DNA. Besides, the ratio fluorescent probe was constructed on the basis of in-situ formation of GQD-CuNC nanohybrid which means no need of fluorescence dye label and fluorescence quencher, dramatically promoting the simplicity of the assay. It is believed that our strategy has the potential to offer a new method for monitoring trace DNA or RNA, and give an effective assessment in clinical diagnosis and biomedical research. However, the assay for target DNA detection also exhibit several limitations, such as the requirement for the design of nucleic acids sequence and the assistance of extra enzyme. Therefore, the pursuit of simple and more convenient assay for the detection DNA was remain needed in the future research work.

Junyang Chen: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Mengke Wang: Formal analysis, Software. Xiaobin Zhou: Formal analysis. Yixin Nie: Formal analysis. Xingguang Su: Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775052 and No. 21575048), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20180414013GH).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128847.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Affinity-mediated homogeneous electrochemical aptasensor on a graphene platform for ultrasensitive biomolecule detection via exonuclease-assisted target-analog recycling amplification
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Ge, W. Wang, X. Sun, T. Hou, F. Li, Affinity-Mediated Homogeneous Electrochemical Aptasensor on a Graphene Platform for Ultrasensitive Biomolecule Detection via Exonuclease-Assisted Target-Analog Recycling Amplification, Analytical Chemistry, 88(2016) 2212-2219.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive surface plasmon resonance biosensor for the detection of HIV-related DNA based on dynamic and structural DNA nanodevices
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Diao, M. Tang, S. Ding, X. Li, W. Cheng, F. Mo, et al., Highly sensitive surface plasmon resonance biosensor for the detection of HIV-related DNA based on dynamic and structural DNA nanodevices, 100(2018) 228-234.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DNA G-quadruplexes in the human genome: detection, functions and therapeutic potential
Nat. Rev. Mol. Cell Biol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Hansel-Hertsch, M. Di Antonio, S. Balasubramanian, DNA G-quadruplexes in the human genome: detection, functions and therapeutic potential, Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology, 18(2017) 279-284.

Clin. Cancer Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.A. Shaw, D.S. Guttery, A. Hills, D. Fernandez-Garcia, K. Page, B.M. Rosales, et al., Mutation Analysis of Cell-Free DNA and Single Circulating Tumor Cells in Metastatic Breast Cancer Patients with High Circulating Tumor Cell Counts, Clinical Cancer Research, 23(2017) 88-96.

Anal. Chem.

J. Infect. Dis.

Zhu, Ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical biosensor for the detection of HTLV-I DNA: A cascade signal amplification strategy integrating lambda-exonuclease aided target recycling with hybridization chain reaction and enzyme catalysis, Biosensors & Bioelectronics, 109(2018) 190-196.

Zhu, "Signal-On" Photoelectrochemical Biosensor for Sensitive Detection of Human T-Cell Lymphotropic Virus Type II DNA: Dual Signal Amplification Strategy Integrating Enzymatic Amplification with Terminal Deoxynucleotidyl Transferase-Mediated Extension, Analytical Chemistry, 87(2015) 4949-4956.

Sens. Actuators B-Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel amplified electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on Au NPs@PDA@CuInZnS QDs nanocomposites for ultrasensitive detection of p53 gene
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, X. Chen, Q. Ma, A novel amplified electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on Au NPs@PDA@CuInZnS QDs nanocomposites for ultrasensitive detection of p53 gene, Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 117(2018) 240-5.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Junyang Chen is pursuing his PhD study under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). His research is mainly focused on the synthesis of quantum dots and mental nanoclusters and their application on sensing.

Mengke Wang is currently carrying out her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is mainly focused on the mental nanoclusters and quantum dots and their applications for biosensors.

Xiaobin Zhou is pursuing his PhD study under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). His research is mainly focused on the synthesis of single-atom nanozyme and their application on sensing.

Yixin Nie is pursuing his PhD study under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is mainly focused on the application in QDs-based electrochemiluminescence sensor.

Xingguang Su is a professor at the Department of Analytical Chemistry at the College of Chemistry, Jilin University. She received her MS degree from Jilin University (China) in 1992 and her PhD degree from Jilin University (China) in 1999. Her research focuses on the synthesis, characterization, functionalization and application of quantum dots and quantum dots-tagged microspheres in biomedicine.
